prompt: Trace the evolution from Java Servlets to the Spring Boot framework. Explain the problems each iteration aimed to solve, and detail the core functionalities of the Spring framework along with essential knowledge required for developers working with it.
article:
## 1) Before Servlets: CGI’s mental model and why Java needed something else

Early web backends commonly used **CGI (Common Gateway Interface)**: the web server spawned a program per request (or used variants like FastCGI), passed request data via environment variables/stdin, and read response bytes from stdout. The Servlet API deliberately preserved this *conceptual* model—e.g., `HttpServletRequest` methods explicitly map to CGI variables such as `AUTH_TYPE`, `REQUEST_METHOD`, and `PATH_INFO` [[8]](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-9.0-doc/servletapi/javax/servlet/http/HttpServletRequest.html)—but moved execution into a **managed, long-running JVM environment**.

Motivations included performance and resource reuse: servlets were studied as a distinct alternative to CGI in performance comparisons of the era (CGI vs FastCGI vs servlets) [[10]](https://web.cs.wpi.edu/~claypool/papers/cgi-perf/cgi-perf.pdf).

---

## 2) Java Servlets (mid/late 1990s): what they introduced, and what pain they created

### 2.1 Key ideas Servlets solved (vs CGI)

**(A) Container-managed components, not per-request processes**
- The Servlet spec defines a servlet as a **web component managed by a container** that generates dynamic content [[5]](https://fazenda.pbh.gov.br/siatu/documentos/siatu/download/servlet2_2-spec.pdf).
- A **servlet container** provides network services, decodes MIME requests, formats MIME responses, and **manages servlet lifecycle** [[5]](https://fazenda.pbh.gov.br/siatu/documentos/siatu/download/servlet2_2-spec.pdf).

**(B) A standardized request/response object model**
- The container **creates** `HttpServletRequest` and `HttpServletResponse` and passes them to `service()` / `doGet()` / `doPost()` methods [[8]](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-9.0-doc/servletapi/javax/servlet/http/HttpServletRequest.html)[[9]](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-5.5-doc/servletapi/javax/servlet/http/HttpServletResponse.html).

**(C) Portable deployment packaging and routing**
- “Web applications” packaged as **WARs** or directories are deployed under **contexts**; routing selects the context by longest URI prefix match, then selects a servlet via servlet mappings [[7]](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-9.0-doc/config/context.html).
- Standardized deployment descriptors (`/WEB-INF/web.xml`) and servlet mappings became central to configuration [[5]](https://fazenda.pbh.gov.br/siatu/documentos/siatu/download/servlet2_2-spec.pdf)[[7]](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-9.0-doc/config/context.html)[[15]](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E13222_01/wls/docs70/webapp/webappdeployment.html).

### 2.2 How servlets evolved operationally: `web.xml`, WARs, and container configuration

The early Java web model centered on:
- **WAR packaging** and a standardized webapp directory structure [[7]](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-9.0-doc/config/context.html).
- **`web.xml`** as the portable J2EE deployment descriptor, defined by the Servlet spec (2.3 referenced by WebLogic documentation) [[15]](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E13222_01/wls/docs70/webapp/webappdeployment.html), and described as spec-defined by IBM as well [[14]](https://www.ibm.com/docs/en/was/9.0.5).
- Containers still introduced extra operational surface area: Tomcat documents multiple places to configure a `Context` and warns against putting contexts in `server.xml` because of restart/reload and override behavior [[7]](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-9.0-doc/config/context.html).

### 2.3 Core servlet-era pain points (what developers struggled with)

**(1) Boilerplate and low-level HTTP handling**
You manually parse parameters, handle encoding, cookies, status codes, error handling, etc., directly through `HttpServletRequest`/`HttpServletResponse` [[8]](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-9.0-doc/servletapi/javax/servlet/http/HttpServletRequest.html)[[9]](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-5.5-doc/servletapi/javax/servlet/http/HttpServletResponse.html).

**(2) Concurrency and thread-safety pitfalls**
- Servlet environments are concurrent; the Servlet spec explicitly requires developers to understand **concurrency considerations** [[13]](https://javaee.github.io/servlet-spec/downloads/servlet-3.1/Final/servlet-3_1-final.pdf), and the spec contains dedicated discussion of **multithreading and thread safety** [[12]](https://download.oracle.com/otn-pub/jcp/servlet-3.0-fr-eval-oth-JSpec/servlet-3_0-final-spec.pdf).
- Historical attempts to simplify concurrency like `SingleThreadModel` were later deprecated [[13]](https://javaee.github.io/servlet-spec/downloads/servlet-3.1/Final/servlet-3_1-final.pdf), reinforcing that concurrency correctness is on the application developer.

**(3) Configuration and deployment friction**
- `web.xml` portability helped, but the descriptor-heavy approach and server-specific descriptors (e.g., `weblogic.xml`) added complexity in real deployments [[15]](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E13222_01/wls/docs70/webapp/webappdeployment.html).
- Vendors and containers often provided extra knobs and alternate descriptors (e.g., Tomcat’s `altDDName` to override `web.xml`) which increased configuration permutations [[7]](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-9.0-doc/config/context.html).

**(4) Tight coupling to container-managed infrastructure**
Your code is invoked by the container, depends on container-created request/response objects [[8]](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-9.0-doc/servletapi/javax/servlet/http/HttpServletRequest.html)[[9]](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-5.5-doc/servletapi/javax/servlet/http/HttpServletResponse.html), and depends on environment setup (security role mapping, deployment settings) that may be handled by a separate deployer role [[13]](https://javaee.github.io/servlet-spec/downloads/servlet-3.1/Final/servlet-3_1-final.pdf). This division can complicate reproducibility across environments.

---

## 3) JSP (late 1990s): separating markup from Java (partially), and why scriptlets became a trap

### 3.1 What JSP aimed to solve (vs servlets)

Generating HTML via `PrintWriter.println(...)` in servlets quickly became “a maintenance nightmare” because UI changes required editing Java code, recompiling, redeploying [[18]](https://docs.huihoo.com/apache/struts/starting-struts2-english.pdf). **JSP** inverted the relationship: HTML first, with embedded Java.

Oracle’s description: JSP pages mix HTML with bits of Java, are compiled into servlets by a JSP engine, and recompiled when changed [[17]](https://www.oracle.com/technical-resources/articles/javase/servlets-jsp.html). JSP and servlets were positioned to “separate presentation from content” [[17]](https://www.oracle.com/technical-resources/articles/javase/servlets-jsp.html).

### 3.2 What JSP introduced as new problems

**(1) Scriptlets mixed concerns and became unstructured**
The same Struts-era retrospective notes early JSP pages often held both request-processing logic and presentation logic; without normal Java class structure, code was duplicated or became tangled [[18]](https://docs.huihoo.com/apache/struts/starting-struts2-english.pdf). Oracle also warns scriptlets lead to complex applications that are difficult to reuse and maintain [[17]](https://www.oracle.com/technical-resources/articles/javase/servlets-jsp.html).

**(2) A push toward “scriptlet-free” views**
JSP 2.0 explicitly enabled a “script-free programming style” [[19]](https://www.oracle.com/technical-resources/articles/javase/code-convention.html), reflecting the industry reaction to scriptlet overuse.

---

## 4) Tag libraries and JSTL: making views declarative (and more designer-friendly)

### 4.1 The problem: scriptlets required Java fluency

JSTL and custom tags targeted the reality that many page authors are not programmers. The Jakarta Standard Tag Library spec explicitly states one main difficulty of JSP is the need for a scripting language; tag-based control flow is “more natural to page authors” [[21]](https://jakarta.ee/specifications/tags/2.0/jakarta-tags-spec-2.0).

Oracle’s best-practices article frames custom tags as a way to eliminate scriptlets and reduce the need for Java syntax in pages [[17]](https://www.oracle.com/technical-resources/articles/javase/servlets-jsp.html), and recommends JSTL to avoid “reinventing the wheel” for common tasks [[17]](https://www.oracle.com/technical-resources/articles/javase/servlets-jsp.html). Oracle’s coding conventions similarly recommend JSTL from JSP 1.2 onward to reduce scriptlets [[19]](https://www.oracle.com/technical-resources/articles/javase/code-convention.html).

### 4.2 Tradeoffs / limitations introduced

As stacks combined, subtle lifecycle conflicts emerged. JSTL 1.2 maintenance notes show non-trivial semantics needed to align JSTL iteration tags with JSF deferred values, warning of **undefined behavior** if the collection size differs between JSF component tree creation and JSP execution [[22]](https://jcp.org/aboutJava/communityprocess/maintenance/jsr052/jstl-1_2-mr-changeLog_PFD.html). This is a concrete example of framework composition increasing complexity.

---

## 5) JSF: component-based UI and lifecycle/state complexity

### 5.1 What JSF aimed to solve

As pages became richer (multiple forms, dynamic widgets), “a page was no longer the logical separation,” motivating **component-based frameworks** that bind UI components to server-side classes and events [[18]](https://docs.huihoo.com/apache/struts/starting-struts2-english.pdf).

### 5.2 What JSF introduced

JSF manages a multi-phase lifecycle and server-side component tree:
- The Oracle Java EE tutorial describes phases such as Restore View, Apply Request Values, Process Validations, Update Model Values, Invoke Application, Render Response [[23]](https://docs.oracle.com/javaee/7/tutorial/jsf-intro006.htm).
- It distinguishes initial requests vs postbacks and explains state restoration (client or server) [[23]](https://docs.oracle.com/javaee/7/tutorial/jsf-intro006.htm).
- Developers sometimes must interact with lifecycle phases (e.g., Ajax partial processing) [[23]](https://docs.oracle.com/javaee/7/tutorial/jsf-intro006.htm).

**Tradeoff:** JSF can reduce ad-hoc request parsing, but the lifecycle/state model is complex; developers need deep mental models of phases, postback behavior, validation short-circuiting, and state saving [[23]](https://docs.oracle.com/javaee/7/tutorial/jsf-intro006.htm).

---

## 6) Struts and action-based MVC: front controller, navigation, forms—at the cost of XML and framework machinery

### 6.1 What Struts aimed to solve

Struts popularized “Model 2” MVC: a single controller servlet dispatches to actions; views are JSPs; models are POJOs/JavaBeans [[18]](https://docs.huihoo.com/apache/struts/starting-struts2-english.pdf)[[20]](https://svn-eu.apache.org/repos/asf/struts/struts1/tags/STRUTS_1_0/web/documentation/users_guide.html). This helped:
- Centralize control (front controller) [[20]](https://svn-eu.apache.org/repos/asf/struts/struts1/tags/STRUTS_1_0/web/documentation/users_guide.html)
- Provide consistent navigation via forwards [[20]](https://svn-eu.apache.org/repos/asf/struts/struts1/tags/STRUTS_1_0/web/documentation/users_guide.html)
- Standardize form binding and validation (`ActionForm`) [[24]](http://exclamationlabs.github.io/struts-1.3.10_docs/userGuide/building_controller.html)
- Provide taglibs for i18n and form interaction [[20]](https://svn-eu.apache.org/repos/asf/struts/struts1/tags/STRUTS_1_0/web/documentation/users_guide.html)

### 6.2 What Struts introduced

**(1) A lot of configuration and processing pipeline complexity**
Developers typically wrote `ActionForm`, `Action`, and configured `ActionMapping` in `struts-config.xml` [[24]](http://exclamationlabs.github.io/struts-1.3.10_docs/userGuide/building_controller.html). Struts request processing became a multi-step pipeline, and in 1.3 the default processor uses Commons Chain (Chain of Responsibility) with many commands (locale, no-cache headers, form creation/population/validation, authorization checks, action execution, forward) [[24]](http://exclamationlabs.github.io/struts-1.3.10_docs/userGuide/building_controller.html).

**(2) Binding convenience created security/robustness pitfalls**
Struts warns: any public `ActionForm` property that accepts a `String` can be set via query string—hence the “firewall” framing and the need to carefully constrain exposed setters [[24]](http://exclamationlabs.github.io/struts-1.3.10_docs/userGuide/building_controller.html).

**(3) Evolution to Struts2 tried to reduce pain**
Struts2 (via WebWork/XWork lineage) emphasized POJO actions, interceptors, conventions over configuration, better testability, and DI integration (including Spring integration) [[18]](https://docs.huihoo.com/apache/struts/starting-struts2-english.pdf). But it also introduced its own conceptual stack (interceptors, OGNL/value stack, plugin ecosystem).

---

## 7) “Heavyweight” J2EE/EJB era: container-managed enterprise services—and “pattern layers” to cope with complexity

### 7.1 What EJB aimed to solve

The EJB 2.1 spec positions EJB as a component model for scalable, transactional, secure distributed applications, aiming to spare developers from low-level transaction/state management, multithreading, pooling, etc. [[25]](https://middlewares.files.wordpress.com/2008/02/ejb-specification-21.pdf).

### 7.2 Why more patterns appeared anyway (and what that signaled)

In practice, distributed object exposure created:
- Tight coupling between clients and business object interfaces
- Too many remote calls (“chattiness”) hurting performance
- Lack of a uniform client access strategy [[26]](https://www.oracle.com/java/technologies/session-facade.html)

**Session Facade** was a formal remedy: expose coarse-grained workflows via a session bean facade, encapsulating complexity and reducing remote call count—but it introduces an extra “business-tier controller layer” [[26]](https://www.oracle.com/java/technologies/session-facade.html).

**Business Delegate** similarly hides remoteness/lookup/exception translation but “introduces an additional layer” [[27]](http://www.corej2eepatterns.com/BusinessDelegate.htm).

**Interpretation:** the platform provided enterprise services, but developers often had to build additional indirection layers to manage EJB remoting friction, complexity, and coupling—setting the stage for a “lighter” approach.

---

## 8) The Spring Framework: why it emerged, what problems it solved, and its core architecture

### 8.1 Origins and motivation

Rod Johnson’s 2002 book shipped with ~30,000 lines of framework code; he later noted many core Spring concepts already existed: `BeanFactory`, `ApplicationContext`, early Spring MVC concepts, `JdbcTemplate`, and tech-agnostic data access exceptions [[28]](https://spring.io/blog/2006/11/09/spring-framework-the-origins-of-a-project-and-a-name). After the book, Juergen Hoeller and Yann Caroff persuaded him to open source it; the project went public June 2003 and the name “Spring” signified a fresh start after the “winter” of traditional J2EE [[28]](https://spring.io/blog/2006/11/09/spring-framework-the-origins-of-a-project-and-a-name).

Spring 1.0 final released March 24, 2004 [[31]](https://spring.io/blog/2004/03/24/spring-framework-1-0-final-released).

A key motivation was avoiding the “heavy-weight, hard to control” development cycle of platforms promising everything [[34]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/2.0.x/spring-reference.pdf), while still enabling enterprise needs: transactions, remoting, persistence, MVC, AOP [[34]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/2.0.x/spring-reference.pdf). Martin Fowler described the early-2000s “rush of lightweight containers” as a reaction to heavyweight J2EE complexity and popularized the term “Dependency Injection” in early 2004 [[35]](https://martinfowler.com/articles/injection.html), which Spring’s own docs reference in their explanation of IoC/DI [[36]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/2.0.0/reference/introduction.html).

### 8.2 Spring’s “big bet”: POJOs + IoC/DI + AOP to deliver enterprise services without EJB-style intrusion

**Core claim (already explicit in Spring 1.0):** you can apply declarative transactions to *any POJO*, even in a plain Tomcat webapp [[31]](https://spring.io/blog/2004/03/24/spring-framework-1-0-final-released).

This was a direct answer to EJB-era pain: get enterprise services without forcing business logic into special component types with descriptor-heavy deployment and container lock-in (themes explicitly highlighted in the “without EJB” book’s structure, including “Deployment Descriptor Hell” and “Testing”) [[33]](https://content.e-bookshelf.de/media/reading/L-586356-1e94967b88.pdf).

---

## 9) Core functionalities of the Spring Framework (what it provides, mechanically)

Below are the core capabilities, emphasizing what they *do* and which earlier-era problems they address.

### 9.1 IoC container / Dependency Injection (Beans, Core, Context)

**What it is**
- The Core module provides IoC/DI; the foundational concept is `BeanFactory` [[36]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/2.0.0/reference/introduction.html).
- `ApplicationContext` builds on it, adding i18n, event propagation, resource loading, and web-friendly context creation [[36]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/2.0.0/reference/introduction.html).

**What it solved**
- Replaced ad-hoc factories/service locators with a formal composition mechanism; Spring describes IoC as addressing the missing enterprise concern of composing building blocks into a coherent whole and codifying patterns as “first class objects” [[36]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/2.0.0/reference/introduction.html).
- Reduced hard-coded wiring and made components independently testable by injecting collaborators rather than looking them up.

**Developer essentials**
- Bean scopes, lifecycle callbacks
- Constructor vs setter injection
- Component scanning vs explicit `@Bean` methods
- ApplicationContext hierarchy in web apps
- Understanding that Boot auto-configuration “backs off” when you define your own beans [[46]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/using/auto-configuration.html)[[64]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/api/java/org/springframework/boot/autoconfigure/EnableAutoConfiguration.html)

### 9.2 AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming) and proxies

**What it is**
- Spring includes an AOP Alliance–compliant interception framework integrated with the container [[31]](https://spring.io/blog/2004/03/24/spring-framework-1-0-final-released)[[36]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/2.0.0/reference/introduction.html).
- It is proxy-based, pure Java, no special compilation, suitable for servlet containers/app servers [[37]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/core/aop/introduction-spring-defn.html).
- Spring AOP intentionally focuses primarily on method execution join points; for broader join points you may use AspectJ [[37]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/core/aop/introduction-spring-defn.html).

**What it solved**
- Implemented cross-cutting concerns (transactions, security, metrics) without polluting business code, aligning with Spring’s “non-invasiveness” principle [[37]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/core/aop/introduction-spring-defn.html).
- Reduced duplication and kept business logic as POJOs.

**Developer essentials**
- Proxy mechanics and limitations (self-invocation, final classes/methods, JDK vs CGLIB proxies)
- Pointcuts/advice basics even if “you don’t need to understand AOP” to use declarative transactions [[38]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/data-access/transaction/declarative.html)
- Ordering interactions among multiple aspects/interceptors

### 9.3 Transaction management (declarative + programmatic)

**What it is**
- Spring supports pluggable transaction strategies and declarative demarcation [[31]](https://spring.io/blog/2004/03/24/spring-framework-1-0-final-released).
- Declarative transaction management is enabled via AOP [[38]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/data-access/transaction/declarative.html).
- Unlike EJB CMT being tied to JTA, Spring transactions work in any environment with JTA or local transactions (JDBC/JPA/Hibernate) [[38]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/data-access/transaction/declarative.html).

**What it solved**
- Delivered a key enterprise capability (transactions) without EJB constraints, applicable to any class [[38]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/data-access/transaction/declarative.html).

**Important nuance**
- Spring explicitly notes it does not propagate transaction context across remote calls; if you need that, EJB may be appropriate [[38]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/data-access/transaction/declarative.html).

**Developer essentials**
- Transaction boundaries at service layer
- Propagation/isolation semantics
- Rollback rules (including declarative rules without EJB equivalent) [[38]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/data-access/transaction/declarative.html)
- Avoiding transaction + lazy-loading pitfalls (esp. with ORM)
- Testing strategies for transactional code

### 9.4 Data access abstraction: templates, exception translation, ORM integration

**What it is**
- Spring provides a generic DAO exception hierarchy for any data access strategy [[31]](https://spring.io/blog/2004/03/24/spring-framework-1-0-final-released).
- `JdbcTemplate` reduces JDBC boilerplate and error/resource handling [[31]](https://spring.io/blog/2004/03/24/spring-framework-1-0-final-released)[[36]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/2.0.0/reference/introduction.html).
- Integrations for Hibernate/JPA/JDO/iBATIS etc., aligned with Spring transactions [[31]](https://spring.io/blog/2004/03/24/spring-framework-1-0-final-released)[[36]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/2.0.0/reference/introduction.html).

**What it solved**
- Removed repetitive try/finally connection management and vendor-specific SQL error parsing [[36]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/2.0.0/reference/introduction.html).
- Made data access consistent across persistence technologies.

**Developer essentials**
- Exception translation meaning and when it triggers
- Choosing between JDBC-style access vs ORM
- Understanding how transactions interact with sessions/entity managers

### 9.5 Web stack: Spring MVC (and later WebFlux)

**Spring MVC**
- Spring 1.0 shipped a strategy-interface-based MVC framework integrated with view technologies [[31]](https://spring.io/blog/2004/03/24/spring-framework-1-0-final-released).
- Spring MVC emphasizes clean separation between domain model code and web forms, avoiding Struts-style `ActionForm` transformation layers [[36]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/2.0.0/reference/introduction.html).

**Spring WebFlux (Spring 5+)**
- MVC and WebFlux coexist; MVC is Servlet-API-based; WebFlux is reactive/non-blocking and was added in Spring 5.0 [[61]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/web/webflux.html).
- WebFlux was motivated by the need for a non-blocking stack scaling concurrency with fewer threads/resources, and by the mismatch between Servlet non-blocking I/O and the rest of synchronous Servlet contracts [[62]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/web/webflux/new-framework.html).
- WebFlux uses Reactive Streams backpressure and typically Reactor’s `Mono`/`Flux` [[62]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/web/webflux/new-framework.html).

**Developer essentials**
- Pick MVC when your architecture uses blocking persistence (JPA/JDBC); Spring explicitly says MVC is usually the better fit in that case [[62]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/web/webflux/new-framework.html).
- WebFlux requires discipline: don’t mix blocking Servlet APIs/filters into WebFlux; it can cause runtime issues [[62]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/web/webflux/new-framework.html).
- Understand concurrency model differences: servlet apps assume blocking with large thread pools; reactive apps assume non-blocking event loops [[62]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/web/webflux/new-framework.html).

### 9.6 Validation (Bean Validation integration)

Spring integrates Jakarta Bean Validation:
- Declarative constraints such as `@NotNull`, `@Size`, `@Min` [[66]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/core/validation/beanvalidation.html)
- `LocalValidatorFactoryBean` bootstraps a provider and adapts it to Spring’s `Validator` API [[66]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/core/validation/beanvalidation.html)
- Method validation via `MethodValidationPostProcessor` with `@Validated`, using AOP proxies [[66]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/core/validation/beanvalidation.html)
- ConstraintValidator instances can benefit from Spring DI via `SpringConstraintValidatorFactory` [[66]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/core/validation/beanvalidation.html)

**Developer essentials**
- Validation groups, method validation exception types, and proxy implications [[66]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/core/validation/beanvalidation.html)
- MVC/WebFlux validation error handling strategies

### 9.7 Scheduling/async execution

Spring provides `TaskExecutor`/`TaskScheduler`, with `@EnableScheduling`, `@EnableAsync`, and annotation-driven scheduling/async execution [[74]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/integration/scheduling.html). Spring 6 enforces that `@Async` methods must return `Future` or `void` [[63]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/wiki/Upgrading-to-Spring-Framework-6.x/10747c0695774ef4dd3c3bbe4f6d4dde96168b08).

---

## 10) Spring as a platform (ecosystem growth): what developers commonly rely on today

Even though your question centers on “Spring Framework” and “Spring Boot,” modern Spring development usually means a portfolio:

- **Spring Security**: for example, OAuth2 resource server support for JWT/opaque tokens, with a defined filter chain architecture (Bearer token extraction, AuthenticationManager resolution, SecurityContext population) [[67]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-security/reference/servlet/oauth2/resource-server/index.html). Boot 3 upgrades to Spring Security 6 and includes behavioral changes such as applying authorization to every dispatcher type by default [[48]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-3.0-Migration-Guide).
- **Messaging/integration**: Boot documents auto-config for RabbitMQ, WebSocket STOMP, Kafka, Pulsar [[71]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/messaging/index.html). Spring JMS provides `JmsTemplate` and message listener containers for MDPs [[73]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/integration/jms.html). Spring Integration provides adapters/gateways for AMQP and message channels mapped to exchanges/queues [[72]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-integration/reference/amqp.html).
- **Testing**: Spring’s `spring-test` supports Servlet API mocks, TestContext caching, MockMvc for controller testing without a server, REST client testing tools, and WebTestClient [[75]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/web/webmvc-test.html). Boot adds focused test annotations and auto-configured test clients, and explains limitations of mocked environments vs real servers [[76]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/testing/spring-boot-applications.html).
- **Observability**: Boot Actuator + Micrometer-based metrics/tracing/observations are now central; Boot 3 auto-configures `ObservationRegistry` and Micrometer Tracing [[55]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-3.0-Release-Notes)[[56]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/actuator/observability.html).

---

## 11) Spring Boot: the next iteration—what it was created to fix, and how it works

### 11.1 Why Spring Boot exists (what “classic Spring” still didn’t solve well enough)

Even with Spring’s IoC and modularity, teams still struggled with:
- Dependency/version sprawl across many libraries
- Large amounts of configuration (XML in earlier years; later Java config but still verbose)
- External servlet container management (WAR deployments, container configuration)
- Production operational gaps: health checks, metrics, management endpoints, secure defaults

Spring Boot 1.0 GA explicitly frames its origin: to “improve containerless web application architectures” and focus on an “ultralight container” suitable for cloud/PaaS deployment, where the platform already provides scaling and management [[40]](https://spring.io/blog/2014/04/01/spring-boot-1-0-ga-released).

### 11.2 Spring Boot’s core mechanisms (and the problems they solve)

#### (A) Starters (dependency bundles)
Starters are “convenient dependency descriptors” that provide a “one-stop shop” for a supported dependency set (e.g., `spring-boot-starter-data-jpa`) without manually hunting artifacts and compatible versions [[45]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/2.1.13.RELEASE/reference/html/using-boot-build-systems.html). They standardize transitive dependency sets [[45]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/2.1.13.RELEASE/reference/html/using-boot-build-systems.html).

**Problem solved:** dependency selection/version alignment and “works together” defaults.

#### (B) Auto-configuration (classpath-driven configuration)
Boot attempts to configure the application based on jars present, e.g., auto-configuring an in-memory DB if HSQLDB is present and no `DataSource` bean exists [[46]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/using/auto-configuration.html). You opt in via `@EnableAutoConfiguration` or `@SpringBootApplication` [[46]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/using/auto-configuration.html)[[64]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/api/java/org/springframework/boot/autoconfigure/EnableAutoConfiguration.html).

Auto-config is designed to be **non-invasive**: it backs off when you define your own beans (e.g., your own `DataSource`) [[46]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/using/auto-configuration.html)[[64]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/api/java/org/springframework/boot/autoconfigure/EnableAutoConfiguration.html). Boot provides a `--debug` conditions report to understand why configurations applied [[46]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/using/auto-configuration.html).

Mechanically, auto-config relies heavily on conditions such as `@ConditionalOnClass` and `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` [[47]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/features/developing-auto-configuration.html)[[64]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/api/java/org/springframework/boot/autoconfigure/EnableAutoConfiguration.html). In Boot 3.x, auto-configurations are registered via `META-INF/spring/...AutoConfiguration.imports` [[47]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/features/developing-auto-configuration.html), and Boot 3.0 removed the old `spring.factories` registration mechanism for the `EnableAutoConfiguration` key [[48]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-3.0-Migration-Guide).

**Problem solved:** huge amounts of manual Spring configuration for common stacks.

#### (C) Externalized configuration + Profiles
Boot externalizes config via properties/YAML, env vars, and command-line args, with a defined override order so environment-specific config can override defaults [[51]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/1.5.6.RELEASE/reference/html/boot-features-external-config.html)[[65]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/features/external-config.html). Profiles allow conditional configuration per environment and can be activated via properties or CLI [[53]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/features/profiles.html).

Boot 2.4 changed config file processing significantly, added `spring.config.import`, and provided a legacy switch `spring.config.use-legacy-processing=true` (not supported in Boot 3) [[52]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-2.4-Release-Notes)[[41]](https://spring.io/blog/2022/05/24/preparing-for-spring-boot-3-0).

**Problem solved:** running the same artifact across environments with clean override semantics, rather than environment-specific builds.

#### (D) Embedded servers + executable apps
Boot popularized shipping **executable jars** with embedded servers (and added reactive Netty support in Boot 2.0 because WebFlux doesn’t rely on Servlet APIs) [[49]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-2.0-Release-Notes). Boot 3’s Jakarta baseline drove embedded server upgrades and compatibility constraints (Tomcat 10+/Jetty 11+ historically; Boot 3.2 adds Jetty 12 for Servlet 6 alignment and drops Jetty 11 embedded support) [[42]](https://spring.io/blog/2021/09/02/a-java-17-and-jakarta-ee-9-baseline-for-spring-framework-6)[[50]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-3.2-Release-Notes).

**Problem solved:** the friction of external container installation/config + WAR deployment workflows.

#### (E) Actuator (“production-ready features”)
Boot includes production-ready monitoring and management: HTTP and JMX endpoints, plus auditing/health/metrics [[54]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/actuator/index.html). Boot 2.0 centralized endpoints under `/actuator`, reduced default endpoint exposure, revamped the endpoint programming model, and adopted Micrometer (replacing Boot’s older metrics API) [[49]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-2.0-Release-Notes). Boot 3 tightened defaults (e.g., only health exposed over JMX by default) and renamed/updated endpoints (httptrace→httpexchanges) [[48]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-3.0-Migration-Guide).

**Problem solved:** operational visibility and secure-by-default management endpoints.

#### (F) Observability (Micrometer Observation + tracing)
Boot 3.0 added improved observability via Micrometer Observation and Micrometer Tracing, auto-configuring `ObservationRegistry` and tracing integrations [[44]](https://spring.io/blog/2022/11/24/spring-boot-3-0-goes-ga)[[55]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-3.0-Release-Notes)[[56]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/actuator/observability.html).

**Problem solved:** standard, end-to-end metrics/tracing that is consistent across Spring components, with better defaults and less glue code.

---

## 12) Timeline highlights: Servlets → Spring → Spring Boot (with major inflection points)

### 12.1 Servlets standardize the web container contract
- Servlet spec formalizes container model, request/response paradigm, lifecycle management, webapps, WARs, mappings, deployment descriptors [[5]](https://fazenda.pbh.gov.br/siatu/documentos/siatu/download/servlet2_2-spec.pdf).
- Thread-safety/concurrency are explicit spec concerns [[12]](https://download.oracle.com/otn-pub/jcp/servlet-3.0-fr-eval-oth-JSpec/servlet-3_0-final-spec.pdf)[[13]](https://javaee.github.io/servlet-spec/downloads/servlet-3.1/Final/servlet-3_1-final.pdf).

### 12.2 Spring (2002–2004): lightweight container + AOP + transactions + data + MVC
- Origins in Rod Johnson’s 2002 framework code (`BeanFactory`, `ApplicationContext`, `JdbcTemplate`, early MVC concepts) [[28]](https://spring.io/blog/2006/11/09/spring-framework-the-origins-of-a-project-and-a-name).
- Spring 1.0 final (2004) includes IoC container, AOP, transactions, DAO support, JDBC template, Hibernate integration, web MVC, remoting, scheduling, mail, etc. [[31]](https://spring.io/blog/2004/03/24/spring-framework-1-0-final-released).

### 12.3 Spring Boot: conventions, auto-config, embedded runtime, ops-first
- Boot 1.0 GA (2014) emphasizes “containerless” executable apps for cloud/PaaS [[40]](https://spring.io/blog/2014/04/01/spring-boot-1-0-ga-released).
- Boot 2.0 (2018) requires Java 8 and Spring Framework 5, adds reactive support, embedded Netty for WebFlux, major actuator + Micrometer shift [[49]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-2.0-Release-Notes)[[41]](https://spring.io/blog/2022/05/24/preparing-for-spring-boot-3-0).
- Boot 2.4 changes config processing (`spring.config.import`, legacy switch) [[52]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-2.4-Release-Notes).
- Boot 2.7 introduces the new auto-config import files; last planned 2.x line [[59]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-2.7-Release-Notes)[[41]](https://spring.io/blog/2022/05/24/preparing-for-spring-boot-3-0).
- Spring Framework 6 + Boot 3 (2022): Java 17+ baseline, Jakarta EE 9+ namespace (javax→jakarta), Servlet 6/JPA 3.1 alignment; AOT foundations for GraalVM native images [[42]](https://spring.io/blog/2021/09/02/a-java-17-and-jakarta-ee-9-baseline-for-spring-framework-6)[[43]](https://spring.io/blog/2022/11/16/spring-framework-6-0-goes-ga)[[44]](https://spring.io/blog/2022/11/24/spring-boot-3-0-goes-ga)[[55]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-3.0-Release-Notes).
- Boot 3.2 adds Jetty 12 embedded support and introduces virtual threads support (requires Java 21 and opt-in property) [[50]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-3.2-Release-Notes).

---

## 13) Essential knowledge for developers working with Spring / Spring Boot (what you must be competent in)

### 13.1 Core Spring mechanics (non-negotiables)
- **DI/IoC fundamentals**: bean scopes, lifecycle, configuration styles, ApplicationContext behavior [[36]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/2.0.0/reference/introduction.html)
- **Proxy/AOP understanding** (even if minimal): why annotations sometimes “don’t work” (self-invocation, proxy type), method-level join point limitations, ordering [[37]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/core/aop/introduction-spring-defn.html)
- **Transactions**: declarative transactions via AOP, propagation/isolation semantics, rollback rules, local vs JTA, and distributed transaction limitations [[38]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/data-access/transaction/declarative.html)
- **Web stack choice**: MVC vs WebFlux, and what the choice implies for persistence and threading [[61]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/web/webflux.html)[[62]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/web/webflux/new-framework.html)

### 13.2 Spring Boot specifics you should internalize
- **Starters**: rely on them for coherent dependency sets; know naming conventions and third-party starter conventions [[45]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/2.1.13.RELEASE/reference/html/using-boot-build-systems.html)
- **Auto-config model**: how it activates (`@SpringBootApplication`), how it backs off, how to debug with conditions report, how to exclude specific auto-configurations [[46]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/using/auto-configuration.html)[[64]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/api/java/org/springframework/boot/autoconfigure/EnableAutoConfiguration.html)
- **Externalized configuration**: property source precedence, profile-specific files, env var binding patterns, and config data imports (esp. 2.4+ semantics) [[51]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/1.5.6.RELEASE/reference/html/boot-features-external-config.html)[[65]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/features/external-config.html)[[52]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-2.4-Release-Notes)
- **Profiles**: activation/inclusion rules and restrictions (e.g., `spring.profiles.active` not allowed in profile-specific documents) [[53]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/features/profiles.html)
- **Actuator + security posture**: endpoint exposure defaults, sanitization defaults, and changes across major versions [[49]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-2.0-Release-Notes)[[48]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-3.0-Migration-Guide)

### 13.3 Testing competence (pragmatic)
- Use MockMvc / Boot test slices for fast feedback, and understand what mocked environments cannot validate (container error pages, some servlet behaviors) [[75]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/web/webmvc-test.html)[[76]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/testing/spring-boot-applications.html)
- Use full server tests with random ports when you need real HTTP/container behavior [[76]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/testing/spring-boot-applications.html)

### 13.4 Security baseline competence
- OAuth2 resource server basics (JWT vs opaque tokens) and filter chain behavior [[67]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-security/reference/servlet/oauth2/resource-server/index.html)
- Boot 3 / Security 6 migration implications (dispatcher types, config style changes) [[48]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-3.0-Migration-Guide)

### 13.5 Observability and operations
- Actuator endpoints and exposure configuration [[54]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/actuator/index.html)[[49]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-2.0-Release-Notes)
- Micrometer registries, common tags, and how Boot auto-configures registries [[77]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/actuator/metrics.html)
- Boot 3 observation/tracing model and how to add custom observations safely [[56]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/actuator/observability.html)[[55]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-3.0-Release-Notes)

### 13.6 Deployment and runtime model
- Executable jar vs traditional WAR deployment choices
- Cloud platform expectations (buildpacks, env-provided configuration), Kubernetes probe integration and graceful shutdown tuning [[78]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/3.2.12/reference/html/deployment.html)

### 13.7 Native image awareness (increasingly important)
Boot’s native image support via GraalVM provides faster startup and lower memory, but imposes constraints:
- Closed-world assumptions; reflection/resources/proxies must be declared or generated
- AOT processing generates source/bytecode/hint files; not all runtime dynamism is supported [[79]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/3.2.4/reference/html/native-image.html)
- Understand limitations around conditional behavior and runtime variability [[79]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/3.2.4/reference/html/native-image.html)

---

## 14) Debates, tradeoffs, and “open problems” across the evolution

### 14.1 “Magic” vs explicitness (Boot auto-config)
Auto-configuration reduces effort but can be opaque; Boot explicitly provides a conditions report for debugging [[46]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/using/auto-configuration.html). Teams still debate whether the convenience is worth reduced transparency in complex systems.

### 14.2 Complexity moved, not removed
- Servlets → JSP reduced Java-in-view pain but invited scriptlet spaghetti [[18]](https://docs.huihoo.com/apache/struts/starting-struts2-english.pdf)[[17]](https://www.oracle.com/technical-resources/articles/javase/servlets-jsp.html).
- Struts centralized control but introduced heavy configuration and pipeline machinery [[24]](http://exclamationlabs.github.io/struts-1.3.10_docs/userGuide/building_controller.html).
- Spring reduced EJB heaviness with POJOs + AOP but introduced proxy mechanics and configuration models.
- Boot simplified setup but introduced another layer of conventions and auto-config interactions.

### 14.3 Reactive vs Servlet stack
Spring’s own guidance is cautious: if your app is fundamentally blocking (JPA/JDBC), MVC is typically best; reactive can help with concurrency under latency but has a learning curve and doesn’t inherently make code “faster” [[62]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/web/webflux/new-framework.html). Choosing WebFlux without end-to-end non-blocking dependencies often yields complexity without benefits [[62]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/web/webflux/new-framework.html).

### 14.4 Migration friction: Java/Jakarta baselines
Boot 3 / Spring 6 required Java 17+ and the javax→jakarta namespace move [[42]](https://spring.io/blog/2021/09/02/a-java-17-and-jakarta-ee-9-baseline-for-spring-framework-6)[[43]](https://spring.io/blog/2022/11/16/spring-framework-6-0-goes-ga)[[48]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-3.0-Migration-Guide). This is a major ecosystem disruption that affects imports, dependencies, and container versions (Tomcat 10/Jetty 11+ depending on era; Jetty 12 aligns with Servlet 6 in Boot 3.2) [[42]](https://spring.io/blog/2021/09/02/a-java-17-and-jakarta-ee-9-baseline-for-spring-framework-6)[[50]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-3.2-Release-Notes).

### 14.5 Microservices vs modular monolith
Boot made it easy to produce many independently deployable services (executable jars, embedded servers, actuator). But operational complexity can grow quickly; many teams are rebalancing toward modular monoliths while keeping Boot’s packaging and ops benefits. (This is an industry debate; the sources above primarily document enabling technology rather than prescribing architecture.)

---

## 15) Putting it together: the evolutionary arc in one sentence

- **Servlets** standardized a portable Java web container contract (request/response + lifecycle + `web.xml`) but left developers with low-level HTTP plumbing and concurrency/config burdens [[5]](https://fazenda.pbh.gov.br/siatu/documentos/siatu/download/servlet2_2-spec.pdf)[[8]](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-9.0-doc/servletapi/javax/servlet/http/HttpServletRequest.html)[[9]](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-5.5-doc/servletapi/javax/servlet/http/HttpServletResponse.html)[[13]](https://javaee.github.io/servlet-spec/downloads/servlet-3.1/Final/servlet-3_1-final.pdf).  
- **JSP/JSTL/JSF/Struts** iteratively tried to separate concerns, improve UI development, and impose structure, but introduced new complexity (scriptlets, lifecycle intricacy, XML configs, binding pitfalls) [[18]](https://docs.huihoo.com/apache/struts/starting-struts2-english.pdf)[[21]](https://jakarta.ee/specifications/tags/2.0/jakarta-tags-spec-2.0)[[23]](https://docs.oracle.com/javaee/7/tutorial/jsf-intro006.htm)[[24]](http://exclamationlabs.github.io/struts-1.3.10_docs/userGuide/building_controller.html).  
- **Spring** emerged as a lightweight alternative to heavyweight J2EE/EJB patterns, centering on IoC/DI + AOP to deliver enterprise services (transactions, data access, MVC) to POJOs with minimal intrusion [[28]](https://spring.io/blog/2006/11/09/spring-framework-the-origins-of-a-project-and-a-name)[[31]](https://spring.io/blog/2004/03/24/spring-framework-1-0-final-released)[[36]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/docs/2.0.0/reference/introduction.html)[[38]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/data-access/transaction/declarative.html).  
- **Spring Boot** then optimized developer experience and operations at scale—starters, auto-configuration, embedded runtime, externalized config, actuator/observability, and modern platform alignment (Jakarta, Java 17+, AOT/native images) [[40]](https://spring.io/blog/2014/04/01/spring-boot-1-0-ga-released)[[45]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/2.1.13.RELEASE/reference/html/using-boot-build-systems.html)[[46]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/using/auto-configuration.html)[[49]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-2.0-Release-Notes)[[48]](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/wiki/Spring-Boot-3.0-Migration-Guide)[[44]](https://spring.io/blog/2022/11/24/spring-boot-3-0-goes-ga)[[79]](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/3.2.4/reference/html/native-image.html).
